title: Refractory very severe aplastic anemia with large PNH clone and monosomy 7 in a 34-year-old female with invasive fusariosis

patient.chief_complaint: Follow-up for transfusion-dependent pancytopenia with persistent severe neutropenia and sequelae of disseminated fusariosis
patient.vital_signs: Temp 37.7 °C, BP 104/66 mmHg, HR 102/min (regular), RR 18/min, SpO₂ 94% on room air.

1.setting: Outpatient hematology follow-up in a patient with longstanding PNH/aplastic marrow failure and recent disseminated fungal infection
1.capabilities: You are seeing a profoundly cytopenic, heavily transfusion-dependent patient in the outpatient clinic. You can review symptoms, exam, current medications, cytopenia pattern, and recent cross-sectional imaging. You decide on urgency and scope of inpatient versus outpatient diagnostics and whether to proceed to repeat bone marrow evaluation and extended infectious work-up.

2.setting: Post–bone marrow work-up visit with new cytogenetic data
2.capabilities: You now have bone marrow morphology, flow cytometry, histology, and karyotype results. You can integrate hypocellular marrow findings, monosomy 7, and the known PNH clone, and reassess the diagnosis and risk profile. You decide whether standard immunosuppression remains appropriate or whether clonal evolution mandates a change in strategy and transplant planning.

3.setting: High-risk marrow failure and MDS transplant-planning discussion in the context of invasive fungal disease
3.capabilities: You have full information including NGS (TET2, DNMT3A), detailed PNH clone quantification, prior IST response, and the course of disseminated fusariosis with ocular and pulmonary involvement. You must define the final diagnosis, antifungal strategy, and an allogeneic SCT plan, balancing curative intent against infectious risk and the patient's reluctance toward prolonged hospitalization.

demographics.valid_request_if: Requests for demographic data, baseline performance status, comorbidities, or social context.
demographics.content:
Age: 34 years
Sex: Female
Performance status: ECOG 1 (limited by fatigue and infections)
Comorbidities: No relevant comorbidities prior to hematologic disease. No prior malignancies, no cardiovascular disease.
Social history: The patient lived with her partner and one young child (3 years old). She worked part-time as a teacher but had been on long-term sick leave. Strong family support was provided by her parents and two siblings (24-year-old brother, 28-year-old sister, both HLA-haploidentical). She was a never smoker, consumed alcohol rarely, and denied recreational drug use.
Height: 165 cm; Weight: 72 kg.

history_physical.valid_request_if: Requests for presenting symptoms, functional status, infection history, and current medication.
history_physical.content:
2024-09-15: The patient presented for follow-up with increasing transfusion requirements, requiring RBC transfusions weekly and platelet transfusions up to 3–4 times per week. ANC remained critically low at 0.1–0.2 × 10⁹/L. She reported skin lesions that showed gradual healing with postinflammatory hyperpigmentation and impaired vision with persistent blurriness and reduced acuity. She reported intermittent cough with occasional blood-streaked sputum and chronic nasal congestion.
Her general condition was poor with pronounced fatigue, weakness, and emotional distress. She appeared tearful and anxious during consultation, expressing significant fear of renewed long hospital stays, remembering the prolonged summer admission for disseminated fusariosis. ECOG performance status was 1.
Current medication: pegcetacoplan 1080 mg s.c. twice weekly, eltrombopag 150 mg 1-0-0, ciclosporin A 150 mg 1-0-1, voriconazole 200 mg 1-0-1, terbinafine 250 mg 1-0-0, valaciclovir 500 mg 1-0-1, cotrimoxazole 960 mg 1-0-0 (Mo/Wed/Fri), sitagliptin 100 mg 1-0-0, pregabalin 75 mg 1-0-1, sertraline 50 mg 1-0-0, pantoprazole 40 mg 1-0-0, filgrastim 30 Mio IU s.c. 2–3×/week as needed, epoetin alfa 40,000 IU s.c. once weekly, lorazepam 1 mg on demand for anxiety or insomnia.
Vital signs: temperature 37.7 °C, blood pressure 104/66 mmHg, heart rate 102/min (regular), respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 94% on room air.

cbc_chemistry.valid_request_if: Requests for current blood counts, reticulocytes, hemolysis parameters, renal and liver function, and inflammation markers.
cbc_chemistry.content:
CBC: Hb 7.8 g/dL, WBC 0.9 × 10⁹/L, ANC 0.15 × 10⁹/L, Plt 18 × 10⁹/L; MCV 95 fL, MCH 31 pg.
Differential: neutrophils 11%, lymphocytes 64%, monocytes 19%, eosinophils 4%, basophils 2%.
Reticulocytes: 12 × 10⁹/L.
Coagulation: aPTT 35 sec, INR 1.3.
Chemistry: Na 134 mmol/L, K 4.6 mmol/L, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, BUN 28 mg/dL, AST 41 U/L, ALT 44 U/L, bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL, LDH 328 U/L, uric acid 6.2 mg/dL, CRP 10.8 mg/dL, haptoglobin 21 mg/dL, ferritin 4950 ng/mL.
Immunology: CD4⁺ 380/µL, CD8⁺ 590/µL (CD4/CD8 ratio 0.64). Immunoglobulins: IgG 840 mg/dL (normal), IgA 68 mg/dL, IgM 42 mg/dL.

baseline_imaging.valid_request_if: Requests for recent ultrasound, echocardiography, CT of sinuses and thorax, or assessment of fungal disease burden.
baseline_imaging.content:
2024-09-18 Abdominal ultrasound: Normal-sized liver (14.8 cm) with homogeneous echotexture, no focal lesions. Intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts not dilated (DHC 5 mm). Gallbladder poorly visualized (postprandial). Spleen 11.2 × 4.6 cm, regular margins, no focal lesions. Kidneys normal in size and morphology (left 10.8 cm, right 11.1 cm) with preserved corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis, no lymphadenopathy, no ascites.
2024-09-18 Echocardiogram: Sinus rhythm, preserved LV ejection fraction (55%), normal RV function, no relevant valvular disease or pericardial effusion.
2024-09-19 CT of the paranasal sinuses: Mucosal thickening in both maxillary sinuses (right > left) and ethmoidal cells, consistent with chronic fungal sinusitis. No fluid levels, bone erosion, or orbital involvement.
2024-09-20 CT thorax: Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (1–1.5 cm), partly cavitary with halo sign, consistent with residual pulmonary fungal lesions. No pleural effusion or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

bone_marrow.valid_request_if: Requests for marrow cellularity, dysplasia, lineage representation, and flow cytometry findings.
bone_marrow.content:
2024-09-22: Bone marrow aspiration showed marked reduction of granulopoiesis and virtually absent megakaryopoiesis. Erythropoiesis was markedly reduced with dysplastic features including nuclear irregularities, megaloblastoid changes, and occasional binucleated forms. Myelopoiesis was strongly suppressed with left shift, hypogranular neutrophils, and pseudo–Pelger-like forms. Plasma cells were slightly increased but morphologically unremarkable. No blast excess (<5%).
Flow cytometry demonstrated a very low cellularity sample with predominance of T-lymphocytes and an inverted CD4/CD8 ratio (0.6). A minimal residual population of myeloid precursors was present without aberrant antigen expression. No evidence of clonal B-lymphocytes, plasma cell dyscrasia, or blast population was detected.
Histology (biopsy) revealed hypocellular marrow spaces (cellularity <10%) largely replaced by adipose tissue. Sparse hematopoietic islands showed residual erythroid precursors with dysplasia, isolated myeloid elements, and almost no megakaryocytes. Small foci of reactive lymphoid infiltrates were present. Iron stain showed increased iron stores and occasional ring sideroblasts (<5%). Reticulin stain showed no marrow fibrosis (MF-0).

cytogenetics.valid_request_if: Requests for karyotype or cytogenetic evidence of clonal evolution.

extended_diagnostics.valid_request_if: Requests for infectious work-up, PNH clone quantification, and whole-body imaging.
extended_diagnostics.content:
Serology for hepatitis B and C was negative (HBsAg, anti-HBc, and anti-HCV all negative), with protective anti-HBs positivity. HIV-1/2 Ab/Ag screening was negative. CMV and EBV serology showed past infection (IgG positive, IgM negative), and PCR assays for CMV-DNA and EBV-DNA were undetectable.
Microbiology: Blood cultures remained negative. Sputum cultures showed no bacterial or fungal growth. The nasopharyngeal viral PCR panel was negative for influenza A/B, RSV, SARS-CoV-2, adenovirus, metapneumovirus, rhinovirus, and parainfluenza. A wound swab from a healing skin lesion grew Klebsiella pneumoniae (KPC producing) and Enterococcus faecium (VRE), but no fungi.
Flow cytometry from peripheral blood demonstrated a PNH clone: granulocytes 68% FLAER⁻/CD24⁻, monocytes 72% FLAER⁻/CD14⁻, and RBCs 46% CD59-deficient (31% type III, 15% type II populations).
2024-09-20 PET-CT revealed FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 1.6 cm, suspicious of known fungal manifestations. No FDG uptake was detected in skin lesions, and there was no evidence of involvement of abdominal organs, bones, or CNS.
2024-09-21 cMRI: showed no evidence of intracranial fungal infection. No parenchymal lesions or leptomeningeal enhancement.

molecular_panel.valid_request_if: Requests for NGS findings and mutation profile.
molecular_panel.content: Mutations detected in TET2 (VAF 21%) and DNMT3A (VAF 12%). No pathogenic variants detected in TP53, RUNX1, ASXL1, IDH1, IDH2, FLT3, NPM1, SRSF2, U2AF1, ZRSR2, STAG2, CEBPA, MECOM.

mrd_status.valid_request_if: Requests for residual disease markers in the absence of blast excess, including clonal markers and PNH clone.
mrd_status.content: No blast excess. MRD positivity for TET2 and DNMT3A. The PNH clone remained quantifiable in all major lineages (granulocytes 68%, monocytes 72%, RBCs 46%), serving as an additional molecular marker for disease monitoring.

treatment_history.valid_request_if: Requests for detailed longitudinal history of PNH, aplastic anemia, IST, complement inhibition, and invasive fungal disease.
treatment_history.content:
The patient had a long-standing history of unexplained cytopenia since her late 20s. In 2018, a PNH clone was detected in the peripheral blood (granulocytes 28%, monocytes 31%, RBCs 12% by FLAER/flow cytometry). Because of worsening intravascular hemolysis, eculizumab was initiated on 2018-07-12 (600 mg IV weekly ×4, then 900 mg IV q2w). Hemoglobin initially stabilized at 9–10 g/dL, but LDH and bilirubin remained elevated.
On 2019-11-30, the patient developed increasing fatigue and one episode of breakthrough hemolysis before scheduled infusion (Hb 6.6 g/dL), associated to viral infection. Due to dissatisfaction with frequent hospital visits, treatment was switched to ravulizumab (loading dose 2700 mg IV, followed by 3300 mg IV q8w). Under ravulizumab, hemolysis parameters improved, but fatigue and reticulocytosis persisted, with LDH up to 650 U/L. Haptoglobin remained <10 mg/dL and hemoglobin 8–10 g/dL.
In 2021, she enrolled in a clinical trial and switched to pegcetacoplan (1080 mg SC twice weekly) after an 8-week overlap with ravulizumab. Complement blockade normalized LDH and bilirubin, and transfusion requirements temporarily decreased. However, progressive pancytopenia developed while hemolysis markers remained controlled. By 2023-04-24, blood counts showed Hb 7.5 g/dL, Plt 25 × 10⁹/L, ANC 0.6 × 10⁹/L. Supportive therapy with filgrastim (30 Mio IU SC q2–3d) and epoetin alfa (40,000 IU SC weekly) was initiated. Transfusion frequency increased to 2 RBC units every 2–3 weeks and platelet transfusions twice weekly. Viral reactivation (CMV, EBV, parvovirus B19, adenovirus) was excluded. Cytogenetics revealed monosomy 7 in 9/20 metaphases.
Because of worsening cytopenia, repeat bone marrow biopsies were performed. The patient was counseled regarding allogeneic stem cell transplantation (alloSCT) but remained reluctant, citing childcare responsibilities. A matched unrelated donor was identified; both siblings were HLA-haploidentical and healthy. A repeat bone marrow biopsy on 2023-08-12 showed very severe aplastic anemia (vSAA): cellularity <10%, absent granulopoiesis and megakaryopoiesis, sparse residual erythropoiesis, and small lymphoid aggregates. Flow cytometry confirmed absence of blasts or lymphoproliferative disease. Peripheral blood criteria fulfilled vSAA (ANC 0.12 × 10⁹/L, Plt 17 × 10⁹/L, reticulocytes 18 ‰).
On 2023-11-11, immunosuppressive therapy was initiated with horse anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) 40 mg/kg/day IV ×4 days, Cyclosporine A 5 mg/kg/day PO (target trough 200–300 ng/mL) and Eltrombopag 150 mg PO daily. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg was added with taper over 28 days. The course was complicated by severe sepsis due to Candida albicans, requiring ICU admission with vasopressor support (norepinephrine up to 1.2 µg/kg/min), non-invasive ventilation, and broad-spectrum antifungal therapy (liposomal amphotericin B 5 mg/kg/day IV + anidulafungin 100 mg IV daily). HSV stomatitis with viremia was detected and treated with acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h for 14 days. She developed acute kidney injury (creatinine peak 3.1 mg/dL) requiring temporary dialysis and pneumonia treated empirically with meropenem 1 g IV q8h and linezolid 600 mg IV q12h. The patient gradually improved, was weaned off catecholamines and NIV, and was discharged from ICU on 2023-12-15. She continued cyclosporine (target trough 250 ng/mL) and eltrombopag 150 mg daily and was discharged home on 2023-12-23.
During outpatient follow-up, no further severe infections occurred, but there was no hematologic recovery: Hb 6.8–7.5 g/dL, Plt 10–15 × 10⁹/L, ANC 0.1–0.2 × 10⁹/L, reticulocytes around 20 ‰. Transfusion dependence persisted (every 3 weeks.
By 2024-06, she developed multiple painful erythematous skin lesions with central necrosis, fever up to 39.5 °C, and cough. CT chest revealed several pulmonary nodules (0.5–1.5 cm, some with halo sign). Skin biopsy showed epidermal ulceration and dermal angioinvasion by septate, hyaline hyphae, consistent with angioinvasive fusariosis. Species identification by TEF1 sequencing confirmed Fusarium petrophilum. The patient was hospitalized and started on liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg/kg/day IV (750 mg/day), voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV on day 1 then 4 mg/kg IV bid, and terbinafine 250 mg PO daily. Empiric piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g IV q8h was given for 7 days; blood cultures remained negative. Gradual clinical improvement occurred, though hospitalization was prolonged.
On 2024-07-02, ophthalmologic assessment revealed bilateral chorioretinal infiltrates consistent with Fusarium endophthalmitis. She received repeated intravitreal injections of amphotericin B and voriconazole, alongside systemic therapy. Visual acuity stabilized at 0.7 bilaterally, with residual blurring. The patient was discharged on oral voriconazole 200 mg bid and terbinafine 250 mg daily, with improving skin and ocular findings.

disease_status.valid_request_if: Requests for a concise statement of current hematologic disease state.
disease_status.content: vSAA not in remission after immunosuppressive therapy with ATG. MDS with monosomy 7 (WHO2022).

special_considerations.valid_request_if: Requests for contextual factors shaping risk assessment and treatment strategy, including infectious history and psychosocial aspects.
special_considerations.content: The patient suffers from vSAA refractory to ATG/CsA immunosuppression. In the context of a large PNH clone, monosomy 7 and newly detected DNMT3A and TET2 mutations, a high-risk overlap situation is present. The course was complicated by severe infectious events, including Candida sepsis, HSV stomatitis, and most recently disseminated Fusarium infection with cutaneous, pulmonary, and ocular involvement. Although broad triplet antifungal therapy has stabilized the infection, she remains transfusion-dependent, pulmonary lesions show FDG uptake and the patient remains profoundly cytopenic, with ANC <0.2 × 10⁹/L. Emotional burden is high, and the patient has repeatedly expressed anxiety about prolonged inpatient admissions. Performance status remains ECOG 1, limited by fatigue and recurrent infections.

round_1.working_diagnosis: Transfusion-dependent aplastic anemia with PNH clone and residual disseminated fusariosis

treatment_plan.action: Arrange repeat bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics, flow cytometry, and molecular panel to distinguish refractory vSAA from clonal evolution toward MDS.
Obtain broad infection cultures (blood, urine, swabs) and restage invasive fungal disease with CT and PET-CT correlation to assess adequacy of current voriconazole and terbinafine therapy.
Administer transfusions (RBC and platelets) to maintain hemodynamic stability and reduce bleeding risk given Hb 7.8 g/dL and Plt 18 × 10⁹/L.
Maintain intensive supportive care with G-CSF/epoetin, strict infection prophylaxis, and close monitoring of ANC and inflammatory markers.
Provide psychological support and re-open discussion about allogeneic SCT as the only potentially curative option, acknowledging the patient's fear of prolonged hospitalizations.

key_findings_quotes: The patient presented for follow-up with increasing transfusion requirements, requiring RBC transfusions weekly and platelet transfusions up to 3–4 times per week.
Her general condition was poor with pronounced fatigue, weakness, and emotional distress.
She appeared tearful and anxious during consultation, expressing significant fear of renewed long hospital stays, remembering the prolonged summer admission for disseminated fusariosis.
CT thorax: Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (1–1.5 cm), partly cavitary with halo sign, consistent with residual pulmonary fungal lesions.

round_2.working_diagnosis: Very severe aplastic anemia (vSAA) with monosomy 7 and large PNH clone
differentials: vSAA with clonal evolution to MDS
Acquired aplastic anemia with PNH overlap

treatment_plan.action: Classify the condition as vSAA with monosomy 7–positive MDS rather than pure aplastic anemia, recognizing the high-risk biology and limited role for further IST.
Escalate transplant planning and confirm availability of a matched unrelated donor, while also keeping haploidentical siblings as backup options.
Continue antifungal therapy and monitor pulmonary and sinus lesions with serial imaging to determine whether infection control is sufficient to proceed toward conditioning.

Maintain complement blockade with pegcetacoplan until a definitive transplant plan is in place, given the large PNH clone and prior hemolysis, while re-evaluating its risk–benefit in profound cytopenia.

key_findings_quotes: Histology (biopsy) revealed hypocellular marrow spaces (cellularity <10%) largely replaced by adipose tissue.
Sparse hematopoietic islands showed residual erythroid precursors with dysplasia, isolated myeloid elements, and almost no megakaryocytes.
Flow cytometry from peripheral blood demonstrated a PNH clone: granulocytes 68% FLAER⁻/CD24⁻, monocytes 72% FLAER⁻/CD14⁻, and RBCs 46% CD59-deficient.
PET-CT revealed FDG-avid bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 1.6 cm, suspicious of known fungal manifestations.
A wound swab from a healing skin lesion grew Klebsiella pneumoniae (KPC producing) and Enterococcus faecium (VRE), but no fungi.

round_3.working_diagnosis: vSAA not in remission after immunosuppressive therapy with ATG; MDS with monosomy 7 (WHO 2022)
differentials: Secondary MDS arising from aplastic anemia

treatment_plan.action: Pursue enrollment into a compassionate use program for fosmanogepix to optimize antifungal coverage in the context of persistent bilateral pulmonary Fusarium lesions.

Plan allogeneic stem cell transplantation as the only potentially curative option, using conditioning with fludarabine 30 mg/m² days −6 to −3, cyclophosphamide 300 mg/m² days −6 to −3, ATG (Thymoglobulin, rabbit) 20 mg/kg days −4 to −3, and low-dose total body irradiation (2 Gy) on day −1, followed by transplantation from a matched unrelated donor as preferred source.
Continue intensive transfusion support and infection prophylaxis, with close monitoring of ANC, fungal disease activity, and organ function to optimize timing of conditioning.
Provide structured psycho-oncologic support and clear counselling regarding expected hospitalization and transplant-related risks, in light of the patient's high anxiety about prolonged admissions.

key_findings_quotes: Final diagnosis: vSAA & MDS with monosomy 7 (WHO 2022).
Mutations detected in TET2 (VAF 21%) and DNMT3A (VAF 12%).

The patient suffers from vSAA refractory to ATG/CsA immunosuppression. In the context of a large PNH clone, monosomy 7 and newly detected DNMT3A and TET2 mutations, a high-risk overlap situation is present.

Given the refractory very severe aplastic anemia with large PNH clone, monosomy 7, and recurrent invasive fungal disease, enrollment into a compassionate use program for fosmanogepix should be pursued to optimize antifungal coverage.
Since the underlying cause of the invasive fungal disease is persistent cytopenia, allogeneic stem cell transplantation remains the only potentially curative option.

Although broad triplet antifungal therapy has stabilized the infection, she remains transfusion-dependent, pulmonary lesions show FDG uptake and the patient remains profoundly cytopenic, with ANC <0.2 × 10⁹/L.
